walkthrough.title: Distribute Quilt Boards
walkthrough.text: Let's get Calico set up. Give each player one quilt board. This is the hexagonal grid board where you will be placing fabric tiles throughout the game. Each board has twenty-two empty hex spaces around three designated spots in the center for goal tiles.

walkthrough.title: Choose Three Cats
walkthrough.text: Now let's pick the cats for this game. There are six different cat scoring tiles in the box, and we need to choose three of them. For your first game, pick any three at random. Place the chosen cat tiles and their matching cat tokens near the center of the table where everyone can see them. Each cat has a specific pattern requirement printed on its tile.

walkthrough.title: Deal and Place Goal Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the design goal tiles. Shuffle them and randomly deal some out so each player can place three goal tiles on their board. Each quilt board has three designated spots marked in the center of hex clusters. Place one goal tile on each of these spots. These goals tell you what colors or patterns you need in the six spaces surrounding each goal.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Tile Supply
walkthrough.text: Grab all one hundred and eight patch tiles and put them in the cloth bag. These are the hexagonal fabric tiles you will be drafting and placing during the game. Each tile has one of six colors and one of six patterns. Give the bag a good shake to mix them up.

walkthrough.title: Form the Market
walkthrough.text: Draw three tiles from the bag and place them face up in the center of the table. This is the market. On each turn, you will pick one of these three tiles to place on your board. The market always refills back to three after someone drafts.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Buttons and Cat Tokens
walkthrough.text: Sort the button tokens by color and place them near the play area. There are buttons in all six tile colors. Also place the cat tokens for your three chosen cats nearby. These are the rewards you will earn during the game when you complete color groups or pattern requirements.

walkthrough.title: Choose First Player
walkthrough.text: Pick a starting player at random. Play will proceed clockwise from that player. Everyone starts with an empty board and no tiles in hand. You are ready to start quilting!

bullets: Select starting player randomly

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Calico is a tile-drafting puzzle game played over twenty-two rounds. On each turn, you draft one fabric tile from the market and place it on your quilt board. Every tile has two properties: a color and a pattern. You are trying to arrange tiles to score points from three sources: design goals, buttons, and cats.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn in Three Steps
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you do three things in order. First, pick one of the three face-up tiles from the market. Second, place that tile on any empty hex space on your quilt board. Third, check if you have earned any buttons or cats from that placement. After you check scoring, draw one tile from the bag to refill the market back to three. Then it is the next player's turn.

walkthrough.title: Tile Placement Rules
walkthrough.text: When you place a tile, you can put it on any empty hex space on your board. There is no requirement to place next to an existing tile. However, once placed, a tile can never be moved. Every placement is permanent, so think carefully before you commit.

walkthrough.title: Buttons: Color Scoring
walkthrough.text: When you create a group of three or more adjacent tiles that share the same color, you earn a button token of that color. Each button is worth three points. You can only earn one button per color, so there are six possible buttons total. If you earn all six color buttons, you also receive a special rainbow button worth bonus points.

walkthrough.title: Cats: Pattern Scoring
walkthrough.text: Cats reward you for arranging tile patterns in specific ways. Each of the three cats in the game has a unique pattern requirement printed on its scoring tile. For example, a cat might need three adjacent tiles that each show a different pattern, or a pair of adjacent tiles with matching patterns. When tiles on your board meet a cat's requirement, you place that cat token on your board and score its points.

walkthrough.title: Attracting Multiple Cats
walkthrough.text: You can attract more than one cat of the same type during a game. Each time you create a new group of adjacent tiles that meets a cat's pattern requirement, you earn another copy of that cat token. Cats of different types can also overlap, meaning the same tiles might help satisfy requirements for two different cats.

walkthrough.title: Design Goals
walkthrough.text: Each of your three goal tiles sits in the center of a cluster of six hex spaces. The goal tile tells you what arrangement of colors or patterns is needed in those six surrounding spaces. For example, a goal might require all six different colors, or three pairs of matching patterns. Goals have two scoring tiers: a high score for fully meeting the requirement and a low score for partially meeting it. If you fail both thresholds, you score zero for that goal.

walkthrough.title: The Color-Pattern Tension
walkthrough.text: Here is what makes Calico a brain-burner. Every tile you place contributes to both color groups for buttons and pattern groups for cats at the same time. A tile that is perfect for completing a color group might be terrible for the pattern arrangement your cat needs. On top of that, you also need to satisfy the requirements around your three goal tiles. Balancing these three scoring systems is the heart of the game.

walkthrough.title: Game End
walkthrough.text: The game ends when every player has filled all twenty-two hex spaces on their board. Since each player drafts exactly one tile per turn, everyone finishes at the same time. There are no extra rounds or bonus turns.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: When the game ends, add up your score from three categories. First, score each of your three design goals at the high tier, low tier, or zero. Second, count your button tokens at three points each, plus any rainbow button bonus. Third, add up the point values of all cat tokens you attracted. The player with the most total points wins. If there is a tie, the player with more buttons wins. If still tied, you share the victory.

3 scoring sources: design goals, buttons, cats

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Quilt
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first several turns of a game, highlighting key decisions and common mistakes. We will use an example player whose board has three goal tiles already placed. Follow along even if your goals are different.

walkthrough.title: Read Your Goals First
walkthrough.text: Before you draft a single tile, let's study your three goal tiles. Each one tells you exactly what the six surrounding hex spaces need to look like. Maybe one goal wants all six different colors, another wants three pairs of matching patterns, and the third wants a specific color split. Understanding your goals before you start placing tiles is the most important thing you can do.

walkthrough.title: Check the Cats
walkthrough.text: Now let's look at the three cats in this game. Each cat needs a specific pattern arrangement on adjacent tiles. Check whether any cat requirements overlap with what your goals already need. If a goal wants pairs of matching patterns and a cat also rewards matching patterns, those spaces can do double duty. Keep this overlap in mind as you draft.

walkthrough.title: Turn One: Your First Tile
walkthrough.text: The market shows three tiles. Let's pick one that can serve a space adjacent to one of our goal tiles. We want a tile whose color and pattern both contribute to our plan. We will place it next to a goal that needs this color in its arrangement. One tile down, twenty-one to go.

walkthrough.title: Turn Two: Building Around a Goal
walkthrough.text: We will draft a tile that fills another surrounding space with the right color or pattern. By focusing early placements around goals, we lock in the hardest requirements while tiles are still plentiful in the bag.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring Goals Until Too Late
walkthrough.text: Here is what happens when you ignore your goals. A new player sees a great opportunity to form a three-tile color group for a button. They place tiles wherever the color match works best, paying no attention to goal requirements. By the time they look at their goals, four of the six surrounding spaces are already filled with wrong colors. The goal is now impossible to score at the high tier.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Goals from Turn One
walkthrough.text: Design goals are your highest-value scoring opportunity. A single goal at the high tier can be worth more than three button tokens combined. Always study your goals before your first placement and make sure your early tiles serve goal requirements. You can chase buttons and cats in the remaining spaces after your goal surroundings are locked in.

walkthrough.title: Turn Three: Spotting a Button Opportunity
walkthrough.text: Let's say we have placed two blue tiles next to each other near a goal. The market now has a third blue tile available. We can draft it and place it adjacent to our two existing blue tiles, forming a group of three and earning a blue button worth three points. This is ideal because the tile also has a pattern that helps our goal.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Chasing Buttons Over Goals
walkthrough.text: A new player spots a chance to grab a quick button. They draft a green tile and place it in one of the spaces surrounding a goal tile, even though the goal does not need green there. Sure, they earn a three-point button. But they just made it impossible to score the high tier on that goal, which was worth ten or more points. They traded three points for ten.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Weigh Points Before Placing
walkthrough.text: Before placing any tile, ask yourself what it costs you. A button is worth three points, but a ruined goal can cost you seven or more. Always compare the points you gain to the points you lose. If a button placement conflicts with a goal, find a different spot for the button or skip it entirely. Goals almost always outweigh buttons.

walkthrough.title: Turn Four: Attracting a Cat
walkthrough.text: Let's focus on cats now. One of our cats needs three adjacent tiles showing three different patterns. We already have two qualifying tiles sitting next to each other. The market has a tile with the third pattern we need. We will draft it and place it adjacent to those two tiles, completing the cat requirement and earning a cat token.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Placing Without Checking Cat Patterns
walkthrough.text: A new player places a tile in a perfect spot for a color group, earning a button. But they did not notice that the pattern on that tile just broke a potential cat arrangement. They had two tiles with matching patterns that a cat needed, and this new tile with a different pattern cut between them. That cat opportunity is gone forever.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Check Both Color and Pattern
walkthrough.text: Every tile has two properties, and both matter. Before you place a tile, check what it does for buttons using its color and what it does for cats using its pattern. Also check what it does for any nearby goals. If a placement helps one scoring system but ruins another, look for a space where it helps without hurting. Always scan color, pattern, and goals before committing.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Poor Color Distribution
walkthrough.text: A new player clusters all their yellow tiles together in one corner of the board, far from any goals. They earn a yellow button, but now the rest of their board has no yellow at all. One of their goals needs a yellow tile in its surrounding spaces, and there are no good spots left for yellow. They have painted themselves into a corner with no way out.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Spread Colors Strategically
walkthrough.text: When placing tiles, think about where each color is needed across your whole board. Your goals each require specific colors in their surrounding spaces. If you dump all tiles of one color in a single cluster far from goals, you might not have that color available where you need it later. Plan your color distribution so groups form near goals, not away from them.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Check-In
walkthrough.text: We are now several turns in. Let's pause and take stock. Look at each of your three goals. How many surrounding spaces are filled? Are you on track for the high tier, or should you pivot to the low tier? Check which buttons you have already earned and which colors might still form groups. Review the cats and see if you have any pattern arrangements in progress. This mid-game check prevents nasty surprises at the end.

walkthrough.title: Before You Quilt Solo
walkthrough.text:
You have seen the core flow of Calico: draft a tile, place it, check for scoring. You have watched common mistakes like ignoring goals, chasing cheap buttons, missing cat patterns, and clustering colors in the wrong spots. Remember: goals first, then layer in buttons and cats where they fit.

Check out General Tips for more strategy advice. Reference the Appendix for quick definitions of scoring terms and tile properties. And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new Calico players make. First, ignoring design goals until the surrounding spaces are already filled with wrong tiles. Second, chasing three-point buttons at the expense of goals worth ten or more. Third, placing tiles without checking whether the pattern helps or hurts cat requirements. Fourth, dumping all tiles of one color far away from goals that need that color. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest traps.

walkthrough.title: Goals Are Your Top Priority
walkthrough.text: Design goals are almost always your highest-value scoring opportunity. A single goal at the high tier can be worth more points than three buttons combined. Before you place any tile, check whether it affects a goal's surrounding spaces. Make your first several placements around your most valuable goal to lock in the right colors and patterns while the market and bag still have plenty of options.

walkthrough.title: Read the Cats Early
walkthrough.text: At the start of the game, study the three cat tiles carefully. Each cat needs a specific pattern arrangement, and some cats are much easier to attract than others. Look for cats whose requirements naturally overlap with your goal needs. If a goal wants pairs of matching patterns and a cat also rewards matching patterns, you can score both with the same tiles. Planning for cats from the beginning is much easier than trying to squeeze them in later.

walkthrough.title: Think in Layers
walkthrough.text: Every tile in Calico has two properties that matter: its color for buttons and its pattern for cats. On top of that, tiles near goals contribute to goal scoring. The best placements serve two or even all three scoring systems at once. When you look at the market, ask yourself which tile helps the most systems simultaneously. A tile that earns a button, sets up a cat, and fills a goal requirement is worth far more than one that only helps buttons.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Market
walkthrough.text: Do not just grab the first tile that looks useful. Look at all three market tiles and consider where each one could go on your board. Sometimes the best move is not taking the tile you want most, but taking the tile that fits your plan best right now. Also consider what you are leaving for the next player. If a tile in the market would be a perfect fit for an opponent's goal, drafting it defensively can be a smart play.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Pivot
walkthrough.text: Sometimes a goal becomes impossible to complete at the high tier. Maybe the tiles you need just are not showing up in the market. When this happens, do not waste turns chasing a lost cause. Pivot to securing the low-score tier instead, and redirect your energy toward goals and cats that are still achievable. Flexibility is more valuable than stubbornness in Calico.

walkthrough.title: Balance Your Scoring Sources
walkthrough.text: The strongest Calico players score from all three categories: goals, buttons, and cats. Do not tunnel on one scoring type at the expense of the others. A player who maxes out on buttons but scores zero on goals and cats will lose to someone who gets moderate scores in all three. Aim for a balanced approach where each tile you place contributes to at least one scoring system.

walkthrough.title: The Mid-Game Audit
walkthrough.text: Around the halfway point, take thirty seconds to audit your board. Check each goal and assess whether you are on track for the high tier or should pivot to the low tier. Count your buttons and see which colors might still form groups. Review cat requirements and see which arrangements are in progress. This quick check helps you prioritize your remaining placements and prevents end-game surprises where you realize a goal was doomed five turns ago.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't ignore goals until spaces are filled with wrong tiles
Don't chase 3-pt buttons at the expense of 10+ pt goals
Don't place without checking pattern impact on cats
Don't cluster colors far from goals that need them

walkthrough.title: Goal Prioritization Framework
walkthrough.text: Not all goals are equal, and the order you complete them matters. At the start of the game, rank your three goals by difficulty and value. The goal with the highest point value and the most flexible requirements should be your primary target. Start placing tiles around it first. Your secondary goal gets attention in the early-to-mid game, and your third goal can be opportunistic. If a third goal is too constrained, accept the low tier and focus energy elsewhere.

walkthrough.title: Overlapping Scoring Zones
walkthrough.text: The strongest boards create overlapping scoring zones where a single cluster of tiles earns points from multiple sources. Look for spots where goal surroundings, button groups, and cat requirements all converge. If a goal needs pairs of matching colors and a cat needs matching patterns on adjacent tiles, place tiles in that shared zone that satisfy both. These multi-purpose placements are the difference between good scores and great scores.

walkthrough.title: Cat Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Some cats are easier to attract multiple times than others. Cats that require only two adjacent tiles with a simple pattern match can be triggered three or four times across your board with minimal effort. Cats that require larger groups of tiles with complex pattern combinations are harder to trigger even once. Identify the easiest cat in your game and plan to attract it multiple times rather than spreading effort across all three cats equally.

walkthrough.title: Button Cluster Planning
walkthrough.text: Earning all six color buttons for the rainbow bonus is a powerful strategy, but it requires careful planning. You need at least three adjacent tiles in each of six colors, which means eighteen tiles dedicated to button groups. With only twenty-two total spaces, that leaves very little room for error. If you pursue the rainbow, plan your clusters to overlap with goal surroundings wherever possible. If rainbow seems unreachable, focus on three or four easy buttons and invest the remaining space in goals and cats.

walkthrough.title: Market Reading and Tile Counting
walkthrough.text: Advanced players pay attention to what has already come out of the bag. If you know there are three tiles of each color-pattern combination in the bag, and you have seen two blue-stripe tiles already, there is only one left. This matters when you are waiting for a specific tile to complete a goal or cat requirement. If the tile you need is likely still in the bag, you can afford to wait. If it has probably already been taken, you need to pivot your plan now rather than hoping.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Drafting
walkthrough.text: In a close game, what you deny your opponents can matter as much as what you gain. Watch other players' boards and notice which tiles would complete their goals or trigger their cats. If a tile in the market would give an opponent a high-tier goal, consider drafting it even if it is not ideal for your board. This is most valuable in two-player games where you see each other's boards clearly. In three or four player games, focus more on your own board since you cannot block everyone.

walkthrough.title: End-Game Space Management
walkthrough.text: The last three or four spaces on your board are the hardest to fill well. By that point, your options in the market may not match what you need. Smart players leave their least critical spaces for last, meaning the spots farthest from goals that do not contribute to cat requirements. If your remaining spaces are all adjacent to goals, you are in trouble because you are at the mercy of whatever the bag gives you. Plan your fill order so the final spaces are low-stakes.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Ceiling Analysis
walkthrough.text: Before the game starts, calculate your theoretical maximum score. Add up the high-tier values of all three goals, six buttons plus the rainbow bonus, and the maximum cat tokens you could earn. This gives you a ceiling to aim for. As the game progresses, revise your ceiling downward as goals become impossible or cat opportunities close. Knowing your realistic ceiling helps you make better trade-off decisions. If a five-point cat means giving up a three-point button, you know to take the cat.

bullets: Rank goals by value and difficulty at game start
Primary goal gets first placements, secondary in early-mid game
Third goal can be opportunistic — accept low tier if needed
Hardest goals first while bag has the most options

entries.definition: The personal player board with a hexagonal grid of twenty-two empty spaces and three designated spots for goal tiles. Each player has one quilt board that they fill over the course of the game.
A hexagonal fabric tile with one color and one pattern. There are one hundred and eight patch tiles total in the game, drawn from the cloth bag and placed on quilt boards.
The three face-up tiles available for drafting each turn. When a player takes a tile, one new tile is drawn from the bag to refill the market back to three.
The bag holding all undrawn patch tiles. Tiles are drawn randomly from the bag to refill the market after each draft.
A scoring token earned by creating a group of three or more adjacent tiles of the same color. Each button is worth three points. One button per color, six possible total.
A special bonus token earned by collecting all six color buttons. Worth additional bonus points on top of the individual button values.
A scoring token earned by arranging tiles to meet a cat's specific pattern requirement. Each cat has a different point value. Multiple copies of the same cat can be earned.
A tile placed on the board during setup that defines scoring requirements for the six surrounding hex spaces. Each goal has a high-score tier, a low-score tier, and zero if neither threshold is met.
One of six tile properties used for button scoring. The six colors are blue, yellow, green, purple, pink, and dark blue. Three or more adjacent same-color tiles earn a button.
One of six tile properties used for cat scoring. The six patterns are stripes, dots, fern, quatrefoil, flowers, and crosses. Cats require specific pattern arrangements on adjacent tiles.
Tiles that share an edge on the hexagonal grid. Each interior hex space has six adjacent spaces. Adjacency determines both button groups (same color) and cat scoring (pattern arrangements).
A cluster of three or more adjacent tiles that share the same color. Forming a color group earns a button token of that color worth three points. Groups larger than three do not earn extra buttons.
The specific arrangement of patterns on adjacent tiles needed to attract a cat. Each of the six cats has a unique pattern requirement, such as three different patterns or a pair of matching patterns.
The six hex spaces immediately surrounding a design goal tile. The arrangement of colors and patterns in these six spaces determines whether the goal scores at the high tier, low tier, or zero.
The maximum point value for a design goal, earned by fully meeting all of the goal's color and pattern requirements in the six surrounding spaces.
A partial point value for a design goal, earned by partially meeting the requirements. Worth fewer points than the high tier but better than zero.
The act of choosing one tile from the three-tile market on your turn. You must place the drafted tile immediately; tiles cannot be held in hand.
Once a tile is placed on the quilt board, it cannot be moved, rotated, or removed for the rest of the game. Every placement is final.
The central puzzle of Calico: every tile contributes to color groups (buttons) and pattern arrangements (cats) simultaneously, often creating conflicts between scoring systems.
Taking a tile from the market primarily to prevent an opponent from getting it, even if it is not ideal for your own board. Most effective in two-player games.
The theoretical maximum score possible given your goals, available buttons, and cats. Decreases as goals become impossible or cat opportunities close during the game.
